transcript.text: Yo, what's up? Today's daily video is surveillance. What I think about surveillance.
We are seeing the beginnings of surveillance, like, you know, video cameras on every street corner.
If you go to Google Earth and you look over there on the left side, all the little features,
There's one called Street View, and I think it's a new one that they just put.
And everywhere in America, like every 30 feet, on every street, there is a little camera icon that you can click,
And it takes you down into this bubble.
And then you can do a 360-degree view from the middle of the street and look at the front yard of your house.
And it's just crazy.
And so, yeah, some people are going to be freaked out by that.
Like, oh, I want my privacy. I don't want anybody to know what my house looks like.
But I think it's just the beginning of that.
I don't think it's going to happen without people willing for it to happen.
People are probably going to resist it, and it won't be allowed to advance.
But I think it will advance eventually to having every – and maybe it will advance even against people's, you know, the masses' consent.
But I think we're going to a day and age where we're going to have video cameras on every corner.
And a lot of places do have video cameras on every corner.
Like, if you go to – I know University Bicycles in Boulder has a camera that's 24-7 that you can click.
So I think, yeah, you'll be able to just click any street corner and then see a real live – what's going down there.
And then it will all be recorded because our gigabyte power is getting so strong, and it's going to be strong enough to do that.
But I like that.
A lot of people are going to be freaked out by the whole privacy thing.
But, I mean, if you are, why?
Give me one reason why.
Unless you're a criminal.
Because that's what it fights against is criminals.
Like, people can't steal bikes anymore.
Somebody steals your bike, you just go to the cops, and they go and they look at the – at that time your bike was stolen, they'll get a photo of the person stealing it.
And even if the person has a mask on, you can just follow them riding your bike back home, you know?
They went over this way out of this – the view of this camera.
So then they turn on the next camera where they went to, and they follow them, and they can trace them all the way back to their house.
So it'll take – it'll take crime away.
Some people might think, oh, but it'll give the government the power to completely control everybody, like that movie V for Vendetta.
And people are against RFID tags for that, too.
But I made a video before about RFID tags where I like RFID tags because it'll make everything cheaper.
Our GDP will improve.
Our quality of life will improve if our time – if our lives are more efficient.
If we all had a – if I had a RFID tag in me, I wouldn't have to wait in line at the airport.
I could just walk right through.
That kind of thing.
I wouldn't have to worry about getting my wallet stolen or anything like that.
And I believe that the government in America – I don't think this whole surveillance thing and RFID tags would be good for, like, a third world country because the government's over there.
I wouldn't trust them.
But in America, I think the government here – I think we're ready for it.
I think the government here is good enough for that.
I believe the people in the government in this country, the people in power, are generally good people.
Or they're at least good enough to not abuse that kind of a power.
I mean, the people – the kind of fascination, the kind of imaginations people have about the negative consequences of surveillance is just unrealistic.
We're not going to go back to that whole Nazi, fascist, Gestapo shit.
We are evolving out of that.
So I really, really like surveillance.
I can't wait for the day where there's no crime.
You don't have to worry about crime anymore because of that, because of surveillance, because nobody would be able to get away with it.

And then after that, you know, people who are disenfranchised, poor people who think they're not – they're getting the short end of the stick from the rich people can turn to other means, more peaceful means to make their point known, you know.
Like, there's all – you know, start showing up at the voting poll.
Less than half of the people in America even vote.
So you can't blame the government if you don't even vote for them.
Sign petitions.
You can make your own petition on a petition site, make YouTube videos.
People are getting more and more open to sharing.
More and more people are opening up YouTube accounts, making YouTube videos.
I envision a day where there's so much surveillance that there'll be a plugged-in video camera in every single room in America.
So you can be like – you can do a YouTube video and hear somebody talk about something or somebody working out or something, and then you go, oh, wow, where do you live?
Or maybe it's even written right there where they live.
So then you can type in their address, room number three.
They live in room number three, and then boom, you can see what they're doing at that moment.
Oh, look, he's working out again.
I knew he would be doing that.
Of course, they could turn the camera off if they wanted to.
But I think people like attention.
Me, I'm kind of neutral about it, but I do believe – I want to play my part in expanding people's habits of sharing what they know, how they live their lives.
So the more we share, the more creative we'll be, the more motivated we'll be to do what we want to do because we'll know – we will have examples for every single kind of lifestyle.
And then if we talk and share our ideas, then we're all going to eventually talk about – argue about stuff and figure out what the truth is via that.
So it all goes down to surveillance.
It starts there.
It starts by people not being afraid of other people.
So my question is why would you feel like your privacy is being invaded by, say, street view or by video cameras on every corner?
I would like to hear a reason for that because at the end of the day, I don't think there is a reason that makes sense.
It's just paranoia.
I upload videos on YouTube all the time.
Yeah, and I get – people are pissed off at me all the time.
But I love it because they give – sometimes they give you reasons for it.
And then you can argue that and then it spans your mind because it – you have to think of new reasons.
And then it fortifies what your beliefs are and all that.
So the whole surveillance thing is just a whole – it's just another step.
And plugging in the world, making the world like one living brain.
Us humans can be like – human species can be like one living brain with each individual as a different cell, brain cell.
If we all hooked up to the internet, the world – I think the brain has like the same amount of brain cells as individuals in the world.
So it would be like having a coherent thinking brain, like one world literally, one species.
So I would say if you're like that and you're freaked out about surveillance, don't be.
If you're a criminal, maybe you should get a job.
Because we're just at the beginning.
They're going to install those – I think they've already installed them street cameras in England and stuff.
And I think it's good. I like it.
